questions.text: Which prominent leader fought against the apartheid system in South Africa?
questions.explanation: Nelson Mandela was the most prominent leader of the anti-apartheid movement and spent 27 years in prison.

questions.text: 'Apartheid' was a system of racial discrimination unique to:
questions.explanation: Apartheid was a policy of institutionalized racial segregation and discrimination in South Africa.

questions.text: When did South Africa become a democratic country with a new flag?
questions.explanation: At the stroke of midnight on 26 April 1994, the new national flag of the Republic of South Africa was unfurled.

questions.text: The umbrella organization that led the struggle against the policies of segregation in South Africa was:
questions.explanation: The ANC was the primary force that unified the struggle against apartheid.

questions.text: What is a Constitution?
options: A set of written rules accepted by all people living in a country
questions.explanation: A constitution is the supreme law that determines the relationship among people and between the people and government.

questions.text: Who was the Chairman of the Drafting Committee of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar is often called the 'Father of the Indian Constitution' for his role as the chairman of the Drafting Committee.

questions.text: The Indian Constitution was adopted on:
questions.explanation: The Constituent Assembly adopted the Constitution on 26 November 1949, but it came into effect on 26 January 1950.

questions.text: Why do we celebrate Republic Day on 26 January?
options: It is the day India got independence
It is the day the Constitution came into effect
It is the birthday of B.R. Ambedkar
It is the day the first election was held
questions.explanation: 26 January was chosen as Republic Day to mark the anniversary of 'Purna Swaraj' (1930).

questions.text: The preamble of the Constitution is:
options: An introduction containing the philosophy of the Constitution
questions.explanation: The Preamble is like a poem on democracy; it contains the values that inspired the freedom struggle.

questions.text: Which of these terms in the Preamble means that people have the supreme right to make decisions?
questions.explanation: Sovereignty means the nation is free to take its own decisions without external interference.

questions.text: A 'Republic' is a form of government where:
options: The head of the state is an elected person
questions.explanation: In a republic, the head of state is elected and not a hereditary monarch.

questions.text: Who was the President of the Constituent Assembly of India?
questions.explanation: Dr. Rajendra Prasad was the President of the Assembly, whereas Ambedkar headed the drafting committee.

questions.text: The Constituent Assembly that wrote the Indian Constitution had ________ members.
questions.explanation: The Assembly was originally larger, but after the partition of India, the Indian Constituent Assembly had 299 members.

questions.text: In which year did Motilal Nehru and eight other Congress leaders draft a constitution for India?
questions.explanation: The 1928 Nehru Report was one of the earliest attempts to draft a constitution for India.

questions.text: Which basic value was NOT part of the 1928 and 1931 resolutions?
questions.explanation: India never adopted dual citizenship; we follow single citizenship.

questions.text: Many of our leaders were inspired by the ________ Revolution.
questions.explanation: Leaders were inspired by French ideals of liberty, the Bill of Rights in US, and social equality in Russia.

questions.text: The system of apartheid in South Africa was particularly ________ for the blacks.
questions.explanation: Blacks were forbidden from living in white areas and had separate schools, hospitals, and transport.

questions.text: A 'Socialist' state is one where:
options: Wealth is generated socially and shared equally
questions.explanation: The Preamble states that wealth should be shared equally by society to reduce inequalities.

questions.text: The term 'Secular' in the Indian Preamble means:
options: Citizens have complete freedom to follow any religion
questions.explanation: Secularism means the state has no official religion and treats all religions equally.

questions.text: Constitutional amendments are:
options: Changes made by the supreme legislative body
questions.explanation: Amendments are made to keep the constitution updated with the changing needs of the people.

questions.text: Which of these features did the new South African constitution offer its citizens?
options: No right to vote for minorities
questions.explanation: The South African constitution is one of the most progressive in the world, emphasizing human rights.

questions.text: The 'Constituent Assembly Debates' consist of ________ volumes.
questions.explanation: The proceedings were recorded in 12 bulky volumes to preserve the rationale behind every provision.

questions.text: How many years did it take to complete the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: It took exactly 2 years, 11 months, and 18 days.

questions.text: The values that inspired the freedom struggle form the foundation for:
questions.explanation: These values are embedded in the Preamble of the Indian Constitution.

questions.text: What does 'Justice' in the Preamble refer to?
questions.explanation: The Constitution seeks to ensure justice in all spheres of life without discrimination.

questions.text: 'Liberty' in the Preamble means:
options: Freedom to do whatever one wants
Absence of unreasonable restrictions on thought and expression
questions.explanation: It means the freedom to think, express, and follow through on one's ideas within the law.

questions.text: 'Equality' in the Preamble means that all are equal before the law and government should ensure:
questions.explanation: The government is mandated to provide equal opportunities to all citizens.

questions.text: 'Fraternity' in the Preamble means:
questions.explanation: It means we should behave as if we are members of the same family.

questions.text: Which country's constitution did the Indian makers look at for 'Parliamentary Democracy'?
questions.explanation: The British model of parliamentary government was a major influence on India's system.

questions.text: Nelson Mandela's autobiography is titled:
options: The Story of My Experiments with Truth
questions.explanation: Long Walk to Freedom details his life and struggle for the liberation of South Africa.

questions.text: Who among the following was NOT a member of the Constituent Assembly?
questions.explanation: Although Gandhi's vision guided many members, he himself was not a member of the Assembly.

questions.text: The draft of the Constitution was discussed ________.
questions.explanation: Every clause was debated and thousands of amendments were considered.

questions.text: A Constitution is needed to:
options: Specify how the government will be constituted
Lay down limits on powers of government
questions.explanation: A constitution serves multiple essential purposes in a diverse society.

questions.text: Robben Island is famous because:
options: The first election was held there
questions.explanation: Mandela was kept in Robben Island, South Africa's most dreaded prison, for 28 years.

questions.text: The resolution of the Karachi session of INC (1931) dwelt on:
options: How independent India's constitution should look
questions.explanation: The Karachi resolution was a key milestone in defining the constitutional vision for India.

questions.text: The word 'Apartheid' means ________ in the local language of South Africa.
questions.explanation: It refers to the policy of keeping races apart.

questions.text: The leaders of the Indian freedom struggle had a ________ on the kind of India they wanted.
questions.explanation: Despite differences, there was a broad consensus on democratic and egalitarian values.

questions.text: Institutional arrangements in a constitution are meant to:
questions.explanation: A constitution is not just a statement of values; it also specifies the working of institutions.

questions.text: Constitutions that are very old often become:
questions.explanation: They are living documents that evolve through amendments.

questions.text: Which of these countries does NOT have a written constitution?
questions.explanation: The UK has an 'unwritten' constitution, consisting of conventions, traditions, and scattered laws.
